notes:
- Key concepts introduced (with brief explanation)
  - Playground: interactive area where you can run sessions with defined teams (like ChatGPT chat sessions) and inspect flow, tokens used, why it ended, etc.
  - Builder (drag-and-drop UI): visual editor where you drag agents, models, tools, termination conditions into teams/agents.
  - Teams vs Agents responsibilities:
    - Termination conditions belong to the team.
    - Models and tools belong to agents.
  - Termination condition types / naming: example uses "approve" as the stopping/approval action (not "terminate"); the stop action must match the configured condition (e.g., "approve" vs "terminate").
  - Prototyping limitations: some features are not fully available in the prototype (e.g., max-terms UI not shown) — intended for low-code prototyping, not full production feature parity.
  - Download/Verify JSON: teams can be downloaded as JSON, inspected, edited; “verify” checks the JSON and will error if invalid/unsupported.
  - Low-code (not no-code): the tool is low-code — you can edit JSON but some edits (unsupported versions/settings) will cause errors.
  - Gallery/templates: pre-made teams can be copied as templates into your workspace and edited/imported via JSON or URL.
  - Local LLM integration: Autogen Studio can be run against a local LM (LM Studio app) for people without API keys.
  - Project architecture pattern shown: backend built with Autogen stack + front end via Streamlit.
  - Multi-agent pattern for literature review:
    - Search agent: crafts arXiv/RF query, calls a tool to fetch papers (fetch more than requested to down-select).
    - Summarizer (assistant agent): produces a markdown summary of selected papers.
    - Orchestrator: a function/coroutine (run_lit / run_literature_review) that runs the team via run_stream, exposing a public interface for the backend.
  - Tool wrapping: use a FunctionTool (wrapper) for custom tools (e.g., the paper search tool).
  - Use of an "RF"/arXiv client/library (referred to as "r python" / "rf libraries") to search papers and return metadata (title, authors, published date, summary, PDF URL).
  - Recommended model/client choices: example uses “GPT 40 mini” (likely gpt-4o-mini or similar) and enabling reflection on tool use to capture title/authors metadata rather than raw output.
  - Best-practice workflow: build team functionally (via code) rather than monolithic script for better reusability and integration with front-end.

- Any analogies or metaphors used
  - Playground likened to "much like ChatGPT".
  - Orchestrator likened to "just like our function" — the orchestrator is a wrapper function that runs the team.
  - "Low code not no code" — positioning the platform as low-code.

- Any code, commands, or config shown (explicit mentions)
  - Download team as JSON (UI action).
  - Verify JSON in UI; editing JSON to unsupported "autogen 2" caused an error.
  - Function/tool mentions:
    - wrap custom tool in a FunctionTool (recommended for newer versions).
    - expose a public coroutine named run_lit or run_literature_review that drives the two-agent team.
    - use run_stream to execute the team.
  - Search tool parameters examples:
    - max_results (default shown as 5, sometimes 10 when testing)
    - sort by submitted date (newer first) or sort by relevancy
    - client.search(query, max_results=5, sort_by="submittedDate" or "relevancy") — paraphrased from transcript
  - Returned paper fields appended into results: title, authors, published, summary, PDF URL.
  - Model choice example: "GPT 40 mini" (transcribed).
  - UI actions: create new gallery, import from URL, paste JSON, copy a team template from gallery.

- Any warnings, gotchas, or important caveats
  - Naming mismatch for stop condition matters: if termination is configured as "approve" but you call "terminate", it won't stop; you must use the correct termination action.
  - Prototype limitations: not all features or parameters are present in the UI; some parameters only available in non-prototype/full versions.
  - Editing JSON to unsupported versions/fields (e.g., "autogen 2") will cause verification errors.
  - Default search result behavior: initially returning exactly the requested number of papers produced poorer results; better to fetch more (e.g., 5x) then down-select the best ones.
  - FunctionTool availability depends on autogen version — upgrade to v0.4.x if needed.
  - Local LM alternative recommended if user lacks API key, but integration requires additional setup (see longer video).
  - The provided examples are prototyping-level — not all production considerations (separate files/folders for agents/teams, full deployment) are included in the simple script.

- Any comparisons or lists (potential table candidates)
  - UI panes/features and purpose:
    - Playground: run sessions, inspect flow & tokens, session history.
    - Builder: drag-and-drop creation of teams/agents/tools/termination conditions.
    - Settings: agent/team settings; some prototype options missing here.
    - Gallery: templates to import/copy.
    - Selector/group chat: selecting teams/agents for group chat sessions.
  - Team vs Agent responsibilities:
    - Team: termination conditions, overall orchestration.
    - Agent: model, tools, system messages, internal logic.
  - Project examples (listed complexity levels):
    - Travel planning — simple example team.
    - Company search — more advanced.
    - Literature review — complex but was simplified in the demo.
  - Paper metadata returned by search tool (candidate table):
    - title
    - authors
    - published (date)
    - summary (abstract/summary)
    - PDF URL
  - Suggested search workflow (list):
    - Build a query (Search agent).
    - Fetch N * requested_count papers (e.g., 5x).
    - Down-select the most relevant.
    - Return exactly the number requested as concise JSON.
    - Summarize selected paper(s) in markdown (Summarizer).
  - Components in codebase mentioned:
    - async/asyncio, typing
    - RF/arXiv client library
    - FunctionTool wrapper
    - Assistant/search agents (team)
    - Model client (LLM client)
    - Orchestrator/coroutine (run_lit)
    - Streamlit front-end
